summary: Drew DeVault documents his process of porting Alpine Linux to the RISC-V architecture using the HiFive Unleashed, the first consumer-facing Linux-capable RISC-V hardware. He explains the two-phase process: bootstrapping (cross-compiler, libc port, base packages) and then porting the full package set. A key challenge was the unfinished musl libc RISC-V port, for which he contributed several bug-fix patches. He plans to integrate the hardware with builds.sr.ht to automate the remaining work and provide public RISC-V build cycles, which he believes would be the first hardware-backed RISC-V cycles available to the public.
key_points: The HiFive Unleashed is the first consumer Linux-capable RISC-V board, implementing an open, royalty-free instruction set architecture
Porting an OS to a new architecture has two phases: bootstrapping to self-hosting, then automating the build of all remaining packages
The musl libc RISC-V port was incomplete and required several bug-fix patches to work at distro scale
Once a system is self-hosting, native builds are preferable to cross-compilation despite slower hardware, because avoiding cross-compile quirks saves more time overall
DeVault plans to make RISC-V hardware cycles publicly available through builds.sr.ht to encourage portable software
money_quotes: RISC-V is an open, royalty-free instruction set architecture, and the HiFive is an open CPU implementing it.
Bootstrapping ends once the system is self-hosting: able to compile itself.
A C compiler and libc implementation open the floodgates to porting a huge volume of software to your platform.
Having a working distro based on the RISC-V port makes a much more compelling argument for the maturity of the port, and for its inclusion upstream.
The time saved by not worrying about this makes up for the slower build times.
Once you unlock the programming languages - C, Python, Perl, Ruby - most open source software is pretty portable across architectures.
One of my core goals with sr.ht is to encourage portable software to proliferate!
key_insight: Porting Alpine Linux to RISC-V demonstrates that open hardware and open software reinforce each other — once the foundational toolchain is in place, the vast majority of open source software ports with minimal effort.
